title: Extending the Prym map
speaker: Samuel Grushevsky
abstract: The Torelli map associates to a genus g curve its Jacobian - a $g$-dimensional principally polarized abelian variety. It turns out, by the works of Mumford and Namikawa in the 1970s (resp. Alexeev and Brunyate in 2010s), that the Torelli map extends to a morphism from the Deligne-Mumford moduli of stable curves to the Voronoi (resp. perfect cone) toroidal compactification of the moduli of abelian varieties. The Prym map associates to an etale double cover of a genus g curve its Prym - a principally polarized $(g-1)$-dimensional abelian variety.

proof_stages.label: Torelli map setup
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls the moduli stack of smooth and stable curves, Jacobians as principally polarized abelian varieties, and the Torelli map from M_g to A_g.

proof_stages.label: Boundary Jacobians and semiabelian limits
proof_stages.summary: By analyzing separating and nonseparating one-node degenerations, the speaker explains why the Torelli map needs toroidal boundary objects such as rank-one semiabelian varieties with a compactified P^1-bundle description.

proof_stages.label: Toroidal targets for Torelli extension
proof_stages.summary: The extension question is formulated for different toroidal compactifications of A_g, with known results showing extension to the second Voronoi and perfect-cone compactifications but not to the central-cone compactification in high genus.

proof_stages.label: Prym map and compactified source
proof_stages.summary: The Prym map is defined from étale double covers of curves by taking the connected kernel of the norm map on Jacobians, leading to a map R_g to A_{g-1} and the problem of extending it over a compactification of R_g.

proof_stages.label: Friedman-Smith obstruction and Voronoi indeterminacy
proof_stages.summary: Friedman-Smith covers are introduced as the fundamental obstruction to extending the Prym map, and the known theorem identifies the Voronoi indeterminacy locus as the union of the closed Friedman-Smith loci with n at least 2.

proof_stages.label: Perfect-cone Prym extension result
proof_stages.summary: The joint work states that the Prym map to the perfect-cone compactification extends to a generic point of each Friedman-Smith locus with n at least 4, suggesting that only the FS_2 and FS_3 loci remain indeterminate.

proof_stages.label: Monodromy-cone combinatorial criterion
proof_stages.summary: The method reduces extension of period maps to checking whether the relevant monodromy cone is contained in a cone of the chosen toroidal fan, with perfect-cone and Voronoi criteria expressed in terms of rank-one quadratic forms associated to dual graphs.

examples.name: Elliptic-curve cusp for A_1=M_1
examples.note: The genus-one case shows concretely that a degenerating period goes to the cusp at infinity and motivates adding only rational boundary points/orbits when compactifying Siegel space.
examples.engagement_reason: He analyzes the genus-one case by identifying M1 with A1, locating the missing cusp at infinity, and computing the SL2Z orbit of infinity as rational points.

passes.reason: the genus-one cusp is a nodal boundary degeneration at infinity showing the naive target A_1 is not compact enough.
the cusp is the boundary nodal degeneration added to compactify the elliptic-curve moduli space, not a refuting example
The unique genus-one cusp is the nodal boundary limit of elliptic curves and is used to show the uncompactified A1 misses the needed limit.
the cusp is the boundary degeneration of elliptic curves to the nodal rational curve, used only to motivate compactifying A_1.
the cusp is the nodal boundary limit of elliptic curves and is used to show that A_1 is noncompact/needs a boundary point
the elliptic cusp is the singular boundary limit at period infinity and is used as the missing-point obstruction to staying inside A_1
the added cusp of A_1=M_1 is the boundary limit representing a degenerate nodal elliptic curve, used only as motivation for compactifying periods.
The genus-one cusp is the boundary degeneration showing A_1=M_1 is noncompact and periods leave the PPAV moduli without adding the point at infinity.
the genus-one cusp is the boundary nodal degeneration at infinity, used to show the Torelli/Jacobian target must be compactified.
the cusp at infinity is the boundary degeneration witnessing that A_1=M_1 is noncompact and must be compactified.
the cusp is the boundary nodal degeneration of elliptic curves used to illustrate the needed compactification, not to refute a claim
the genus-one cusp is the nodal boundary limit showing A_1=M_1 is noncompact and the Torelli target must be compactified

examples.name: Zero-shift nodal rational factor
examples.note: The special case P=Q in the one-node Jacobian limit makes the shift construction concrete by recovering a product of the normalization's Jacobian with a nodal rational curve.
examples.engagement_reason: He applies the semi-abelian compactification construction to the zero-shift case, explaining that identifying zero and infinity without shift gives a nodal rational curve factor times the Jacobian of the base.

passes.reason: the zero-shift case is the split boundary limit with a nodal rational factor, used as a consistency example rather than to refute a claim.
zero shift gives the split product with a nodal rational factor, a singular boundary degeneration with extra product regularity
The zero shift gives the split product with a singular nodal rational factor, a trivial limiting degeneration used illustratively rather than adversarially.
zero shift gives the trivial product with a nodal rational factor, a boundary semi-abelic degeneration rather than an adversarial example.
the zero shift gives the boundary product with a nodal rational curve, used as a consistency example rather than an obstruction
zero shift gives the boundary semi-abelic limit splitting off a nodal rational factor, used only as a special consistency case
the zero-shift case is the boundary product with a nodal rational factor, a degenerate limiting semiabelic/Jacobian object used illustratively.
The zero-shift case is a singular boundary product with a nodal rational factor, used as a sanity-check special case rather than to refute a claim.
the zero-shift case is the singular boundary product with a nodal rational factor, used only as a confirming special case of the Jacobian formula.
the zero shift gives the limiting product with a nodal rational factor, used as a consistency check rather than an obstruction.

zero shift gives the rank-one semiabelic boundary object as a product with a nodal rational curve, used as an illustrative special case

examples.name: Two-by-two positive-definite cone
examples.note: The cone of 2 by 2 positive-definite symmetric matrices is used as a visual special case showing why the positive cone is round and must be decomposed into polyhedral cones for a toroidal compactification.
examples.engagement_reason: He specializes the positive-definite cone to two-by-two symmetric matrices and checks the defining inequalities, noting the determinant condition makes the cone round rather than polyhedral.

passes.reason: the 2-by-2 positive-definite cone is a round homogeneous cone used to show the positive-definite cone is not polyhedral.
the two-by-two positive-definite cone is a homogeneous round symmetric cone used only as an illustrative model
The 2x2 positive-definite cone is a highly regular symmetric cone and is invoked to show the positivity cone is round, not polyhedral.
